FIG \u2014 Banks.lede: Big bank earnings set to boom on SpaceX and volatility; Fed signals rate hikes aren't off table; CFPB gets Trump-friendly leadership tweak.

bullets.text: Q2 bank earnings expected to be strong, driven by SpaceX IPO advisory fees, geopolitical volatility trading, and pickup in commercial lending.
CFPB nominee Brian Johnson wins 60-day review period on staff-cut plan post-confirmation, giving Trump's pick discretion over bureau workforce reduction.
Fed Governor Waller warns inflation may be broadening beyond energy and tariffs, leaving open possibility of further rate hikes despite recent disinflation.

FIG \u2014 Asset & Wealth Management.lede: RIA consolidation hits overdrive with Hightower and Choreo posting strong growth, while advisors eye 401(k) and alternative assets as revenue drivers.

bullets.text: RIA M&A momentum continues: Hightower posts record organic growth, ex-Fido CTO raises $7.7M for standalone platform, and Choreo closes fourth acquisition post-SpaceX win.
401(k) plan innovation driven by revenue opportunity and competitive positioning—advisors treating retirement plans as key growth lever amid tightening margins.
Semiliquid funds gaining traction with institutional allocators; leverage and redemption gates becoming critical valuation tools as advisors manage liquidity-return tradeoffs.

FIG \u2014 Insurance.lede: Brazil's insurance market opens for business, Mile Auto expands via Georgia acquisition, Kenvue faces major litigation exposure, and AI/underwriting tools reshape the competitive landscape.

bullets.text: Brazil's new Insurance Law takes effect with regulatory tailwinds—improved claims handling and contractual clarity signaling market maturation and attracting reinsurer interest.
Mile Auto acquires Insurance House (~$100M premium, 55k+ policyholders) effective July 1, 2026, expanding AI-driven MGA footprint to 10 states and 1,600 distribution partners.
Appeals court revives 500+ private lawsuits against Kenvue (Tylenol) for alleged autism link—major litigation exposure after district court dismissal overturned.
Dun & Bradstreet embeds Claude AI into commercial underwriting platform for real-time risk data; Marsh Risk launches MLOne umbrella casualty facility to streamline excess claims coordination.

FIG \u2014 Specialty Finance.lede: Mastercard explores Vocalink exit; Moneybox hits £800M valuation; fintech partnerships reshape payment rails.

bullets.text: Mastercard is exploring a sale of Vocalink, the UK payments platform acquired in 2016, potentially returning it to original bank owners.
Moneybox conducting secondary sale at £800M valuation (45% jump YoY), raising up to £45M for the UK digital wealth manager.
North America maintains LP appeal in private debt despite market divergence between performing and distressed credits.

PE & Deals.lede: Arctos deploys novel GP financing structure while secondary market consolidates; strategic sector bets in defense and utilities.

bullets.text: Arctos Partners closed a $6.2B GP financing deal using a novel 'self-liquidating' Keystone structure—a first for the GP-financing market.
Moelis is building out LP-led secondaries after hiring an Evercore VP and team, capitalizing on favorable pricing and liquidity conditions driving market growth.
Bridgepoint acquired majority stake in Danish defense-tech UMag (spun from Tech University of Denmark) as European defense spending accelerates; founders retain significant equity.

Markets & Macro.lede: Bank earnings season opens amid Fed rate uncertainty; PE mega-funds dominate capital raising while Wall Street eyes best IB year since 2022.

bullets.text: Bank earnings kick off Tuesday with market braced for high expectations and potential disappointment; results will set tone for quarterly guidance.
Top 20 PE firms raised $519B in H1 2026 (+27% YoY), consolidating power while smaller funds struggle—signaling potential wave of sub-scale consolidation.
US investment banking fees tracking toward highest annual total since 2022 on M&A revival, IPO recovery, and AI-fueled capital markets activity.
Fed's Waller signals rate hike possible if this week's inflation data comes hot; officials divided on policy path ahead.

Newsletters.lede: Big bank earnings week ahead anchors markets; meanwhile, ETF flows turn negative and China's AI advances challenge US tech dominance.

bullets.text: Five of six largest US banks report Q2 earnings tomorrow—critical read on credit quality, deposits, and consumer health across lending and wealth management.
ETF flows swung negative in rare week; redemptions hit equity, bond, and alternative products as investors reassess positioning.
Alibaba's AI model ranks first globally; cheaper Chinese alternatives are eroding US tech dominance in artificial intelligence, signaling competitive pressure ahead.

Career Intel.lede: No stories in this lane today.

Learn.lede: Four stories on competitive dynamics, mental health diagnostics, and policy preferences; one on AI moat erosion.

bullets.text: Open-source AI now matches frontier models at 1/10th the cost, forcing investors to hunt for moats beyond raw model performance—data, distribution, or domain expertise becoming the battleground.
UK young people reporting mental illness surged while functional impairment flatlined—diagnostic creep and labeling shifts, not worsening actual wellbeing.
Economic literacy, not self-interest, predicts EU support for trade and immigration—knowledge matters more than personal gain in policy preferences.

all.lede: Bank earnings season kicks off amid strong macro signals; mega-PE fundraising surges while Mastercard explores Vocalink exit.

bullets.text: Big Six US banks report Q2 earnings Tuesday-Wednesday; results will signal credit quality, deposit health, and consumer spending across all major business lines.
Top 20 PE firms raised $519B in H1 2026 (+27% YoY), widening gap with smaller funds and raising consolidation risks in the sub-scale market.
Investment banking fees on track for best year since 2022, driven by M&A revival, IPO recovery, and AI-fueled DCM activity.
Mastercard exploring sale of Vocalink, the UK payments platform acquired from British banks in 2016, potentially returning ownership to originators.
